summary: Gruber shares a passage from a 1975 Time magazine profile of Stanley Kubrick, found in a book of Kubrick interviews. The passage describes Kubrick as intensely self-conscious and rational about his work, valuing privacy and the efficient use of time to 'create a string of masterpieces.' Gruber draws a direct parallel to Steve Jobs via a linked photo, implying Jobs shares Kubrick's singular focus on craft over social status or wealth.
key_points: Kubrick is characterized as secretive and privacy-obsessed, driven by awareness of time's passage and a desire to create masterpieces
The quote emphasizes that for Kubrick, social status was irrelevant and money was merely a tool — values Gruber implicitly attributes to Jobs as well
The post draws a parallel between two iconic figures in different fields who shared an uncompromising pursuit of excellence
money_quotes: His eccentricities — secretiveness, a great need for privacy — are caused by his intense awareness of time's relentless passage.
He wants to use time to 'create a string of masterpieces', as an acquaintance puts it.
Social status means nothing to him, money is simply a tool of his trade.
key_insight: Gruber sees in Kubrick's single-minded devotion to creating masterpieces — indifferent to status and treating money as mere tooling — a mirror of Steve Jobs's own ethos.
